Comb Filter Documentation

Overview

The Comb Filter introduces delay-based interference effects — notched cuts (feedforward mode) or resonant metallic peaks (feedback mode) spaced evenly across the frequency spectrum. The delay time is modulatable, enabling flanging and chorus sweeps in feedforward mode, or pitch-shifting resonances in feedback mode. A one-pole damping filter in the delay path tames the high-frequency content for more natural-sounding results.

Category

Filters (Purple) - Signal processing and spectral shaping

Core Concept

Feedforward (FIR) mode — produces evenly-spaced notches:

y[n] = x[n] + feedback × x[n-D]

Feedback (IIR) mode — produces resonant peaks (pitched comb):

y[n] = x[n] + feedback × y[n-D]

Where D is the delay in samples (delayTime × sampleRate / 1000). Peaks/notches appear at integer multiples of 1/delayTime, spaced 1000/delayTime Hz apart.

Ports

Input Ports

Port Data Type Default Description
signalIn Signal 0.0 Input audio signal
delayTime Control 5.0 Delay time in milliseconds (0.1–100.0 ms)
feedback Control 0.5 Delay feedback / comb coefficient (−0.99 to 0.99)
damping Control 0.0 High-frequency damping in the delay path (0.0–1.0)

Note: mode (Feedforward/Feedback) is set via the inspector and is not modulatable.

Output Ports

Port Data Type Description
signalOut Signal Processed audio output

Parameters

Parameter Type Default Range Description
mode Integer 0 (Feedforward) 0–1 Filter topology (inspector only, not modulatable)
delayTime Float 5.0 0.1–100.0 Delay time in milliseconds
feedback Float 0.5 −0.99–0.99 Comb coefficient — controls notch/peak depth
damping Float 0.0 0.0–1.0 Amount of one-pole lowpass damping on the delayed signal

Operating Modes

Feedforward (mode = 0)

The delayed input is added back to the direct signal. Constructive/destructive interference creates evenly spaced notches in the spectrum. Modulating delayTime sweeps the notch positions — the classic flanger effect.

Notch spacing  = 1000 / delayTime  Hz
First notch at = 500 / delayTime   Hz

Example (delayTime = 5 ms): - First notch: 100 Hz - Notch spacing: 200 Hz - Notches at: 100, 300, 500, 700 Hz…

Feedback polarity: - Positive feedback (> 0): Notches at odd multiples, peak at DC - Negative feedback (< 0): Notches at even multiples (different comb pattern)

Feedback (mode = 1)

The delayed output is fed back in. This creates resonant peaks at the delay frequency and its integer multiples. The filter rings — it has a tail after the input signal stops. High feedback values produce pronounced, metallic resonance.

Peak frequency = 1000 / delayTime  Hz  (and harmonics)

Example (delayTime = 5 ms): - Fundamental resonance: 200 Hz - Resonant peaks at: 200, 400, 600, 800 Hz…

This is essentially a Karplus-Strong-like resonator without the noise excitation — connect a noise burst or impulsive signal to create pitched metallic ringing.

Stability: Feedback values approaching ±1.0 produce extreme resonance. Values at or beyond ±1.0 will cause runaway feedback (the filter becomes unstable). Keep feedback below ±0.99.

Delay Time and Frequency Effects

delayTime (ms) Notch/peak spacing Fundamental resonance (feedback mode)
0.1 10 kHz 10 kHz
0.5 2 kHz 2 kHz
1.0 1 kHz 1 kHz
5.0 (default) 200 Hz 200 Hz
10.0 100 Hz 100 Hz
25.0 40 Hz 40 Hz
100.0 10 Hz 10 Hz

Feedback Values

Feedback Feedforward Effect Feedback Effect
0.0 No effect (pass-through) No effect
0.3 Gentle notching Light resonance
0.5 (default) Moderate notching Clear resonance
0.7–0.8 Deep notches Strong, singing resonance
0.9–0.95 Near-complete cancellation at notches Very strong resonance, long ring
−0.5 Shifted notch pattern Phase-inverted resonance

Damping

The damping parameter controls a one-pole lowpass filter applied to the delayed signal before it is mixed back. Higher values reduce high-frequency content in the feedback/feedforward path:

Damping Effect
0.0 (default) No damping — full bandwidth in delay path
0.3 Gentle HF reduction — slightly warmer comb
0.6 Moderate HF damping — mellow resonance
0.9 Heavy damping — very warm, low-frequency resonance
1.0 Maximum damping — near-complete HF elimination

Damping is especially useful in Feedback mode to prevent the high-frequency resonances from becoming too harsh. It models the natural energy absorption at the reflection point of a physical system (like a string nut or wall surface).

Usage Patterns

Flanging (Feedforward, Modulated Delay)

[Any Signal Source] → signalIn → [Comb Filter] → signalOut
                                   mode: Feedforward
                                   delayTime: 1.0–5.0 ms
                                   feedback: 0.6
                          [LFO] → delayTime
                            frequency: 0.2 Hz, waveType: Sine

The LFO sweeps the delay time, moving the notch comb up and down in frequency — producing the classic flanger sweep.

Metallic Resonator (Feedback Mode)

[Noise Color Filter] → signalIn → [Comb Filter] → signalOut
                                    mode: Feedback
                                    delayTime: 4.5 ms  (≈ 222 Hz resonance)
                                    feedback: 0.85
                                    damping: 0.3

A short noise burst through a high-feedback comb produces a metallic ping or bell-like sustain.

Pitch-Tracking Resonator

Use Frequency Mapper to set the delay time based on the note’s pitch:

[Frequency Mapper] → controlOut → delayTime
  outputMin: 0.5    (high pitch = short delay = high resonance)
  outputMax: 20.0   (low pitch = long delay = low resonance)
  invert: true
  curveType: Logarithmic

With feedback mode, the comb filter’s fundamental resonance approximately tracks the note pitch.

Chorus / Doubling (Feedforward, Short Delay)

[Signal Source] → signalIn → [Comb Filter] → signalOut
                               mode: Feedforward
                               delayTime: 10–30 ms
                               feedback: 0.4

Short feedforward delays without modulation create a doubling or chorus thickening effect.

Harmonic Thinning (Feedforward, Tuned Notches)

Set the delay time so notches fall on unwanted harmonics:

If fundamental pitch = 200 Hz and you want to notch every other harmonic:
  delayTime = 1000 / (2 × 200) = 2.5 ms

This notches harmonics 1, 3, 5… leaving harmonics 2, 4, 6… (or vice versa with negative feedback).

Tail Behavior

The Comb Filter produces a decay tail (the audio graph recognizes it via hasTail()). In Feedback mode with significant feedback values, the filter continues ringing after the input signal ends. Kala will render additional tail samples after the note to capture this ring-out, so the decay is heard naturally.

Comparison with Other Filters

Container Type Resonance Typical Effect
Comb Filter (Feedforward) FIR No Flanging, notching, thickening
Comb Filter (Feedback) IIR Yes (multi-peak) Metallic resonance, pitched ring
LP/HP Filter IIR biquad Yes (single peak) Smooth brightness control
Formant Body IIR resonator Yes (narrow) Vocal body resonance
10-Band EQ Parallel bandpass Mild Broad spectral shaping

Troubleshooting

No Audible Effect

  1. Check that feedback is not 0.0
  2. Verify signalIn is connected and producing audio
  3. In Feedforward mode with very long delay times (> 50 ms), the effect may be perceived as echo rather than coloration

Unstable / Runaway Sound

  1. Reduce feedback — values near ±0.99 in Feedback mode can cause very long ring-outs or (if modulation pushes the gain above 1.0) instability
  2. Increase damping to absorb energy in the delay path
  3. In Feedback mode, ensure that modulated feedback values never exceed ±0.99

Too Harsh / Metallic

  1. Increase damping to soften the high-frequency resonances
  2. Reduce feedback for less pronounced resonant peaks
  3. Place a LP/HP Filter or 10-Band EQ after the Comb Filter to trim the high end

Flanging Not Audible

  1. Ensure the LFO or modulation source is connected to delayTime (not feedback)
  2. Try delay time ranges of 0.5–5 ms for the most pronounced flanging
  3. Increase feedback toward 0.7–0.8 for deeper notches

Technical Notes

Circular Buffer

The delay line uses a circular buffer pre-allocated for 100 ms maximum delay time. The write pointer advances each sample; reads use linear interpolation for sub-sample delay accuracy when delay time is modulated.

Reset Behavior

The delay buffer and damping filter state are zeroed at the start of non-legato notes. For legato transitions, state is preserved so the ring-out from the previous note carries over naturally.

Documentation accurate as of Kala v1.4. Source files: combfilter.h, combfilter.cpp
